attributes.stem:
An 82-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a two-day history of odynophagia. He reports itching and burning sensation in his mouth and noticed some blood when brushing his teeth yesterday. He has no dysphagia or retrosternal pain. His medical history includes chronic obstructive pulmonary disease treated with fluticasone/salmeterol inhaler. He takes no other regular medications.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. His mouth and tongue are examined (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely explanation for his symptoms?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic features of oral candidiasis (thrush), characterised by white plaques that can be scraped off to reveal an erythematous base. The most likely cause in this case is a medication side effect, specifically inhaled corticosteroid use for COPD. Approximately 5-7% of patients using inhaled corticosteroids develop oral candidiasis, with risk factors including higher doses and frequency of use.

Iron deficiency anaemia typically causes atrophic glossitis with a smooth, glossy, red tongue appearance rather than white plaques. While it can cause oral symptoms, the appearance would be quite different.

Undiagnosed diabetes mellitus can predispose to oral candidiasis due to immunosuppression and altered glucose levels, but without other symptoms of diabetes, the inhaled corticosteroids are a more likely cause in this case.

Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease can cause tongue changes but typically presents with heartburn and acid regurgitation. The tongue may appear coated but would not have the characteristic white plaques of candidiasis.

Vitamin B12 deficiency typically causes a smooth, red, painful tongue (glossitis) and would not present with white plaques.

Prevention of oral candidiasis in patients using inhaled corticosteroids includes rinsing the mouth after each use and using a spacer device. Treatment involves topical antifungal agents such as miconazole gel or nystatin for 7-14 days, continuing for several days after symptom resolution.
learningPoints: Oral candidiasis commonly presents with white plaques and odynophagia in patients using inhaled corticosteroids.
Prevention of oral candidiasis includes mouth rinsing after inhaler use and using a spacer device.
Topical antifungal therapy is the first-line treatment for oral candidiasis.
references: Akpan A, Morgan R. Oral candidiasis. Postgraduate medical journal. 2002 Aug 1;78(922):455-9. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1742467/

Gandara BK, Morton TH. Non-periodontal oral manifestations of diabetes: a framework for medical care providers. Diabetes spectrum. 2011 Nov 1;24(4):199-205. Available at: https://diabetesjournals.org/spectrum/article/24/4/199/31883/Non-Periodontal-Oral-Manifestations-of-Diabetes-A

Mohamed AA, Lu XL, Mounmin FA. Diagnosis and treatment of esophageal candidiasis: Current updates. Canadian Journal of Gastroenterology and Hepatology. 2019 Oct 20;2019. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6854261/

Yang IA, Dabscheck EJ, George J, Jenkins SC, McDonald CF, McDonald VM, Smith BJ, Zwar NA. COPD-X Concise Guide. In: Brisbane: Lung Foundation Australia [online]. 2019. Available at: https://lungfoundation.com.au/wp-content/uploads/2018/09/Book-COPD-X-Concise-Guide-Jun2020.pdf
Oral candidiasis and candida-associated lesions. In: eTG Complete [online]. Therapeutic Guidelines Ltd. 2019 [updated March 2021]. Available at: https://tgldcdp.tg.org.au
